title: Characterization of Evangelion
wikitext:
[]: Characterization of Evangelion             Quote Post by GAP » Mon Jun 18, 2012 1:19 pm It kind of bothers me but what is it about the characters that makes them so appealing and appalling at the same time? I praise them for their best moments, I get disappointed when they screw up, I sympathize with their situtations and I get disgusted with them from time to time. Anno really put a lot of thought into his characters in that they are more than just their archetypes but real people in a very bizarre situation. I cannot seem to find such characterization in Gundam or any other mecha anime I watched so far. It is not just limited to the anime but the manga also seems to have some shades of this. Almost everyone on this forum (and even outside Evageeks) seems to see themselves in the characters and the characters themselves seem to transcend the show. I am not sure if I can never write characters of this quality although I am not on meds or even remotely as depressed as Anno is. It amazes me how cast are both reviled and praised as the same time but is almost no middle ground to them. The main lead Shinji is no shonen hero but fourteen year old who is coming of age yet he seems a lot more 'real' any of them. Maybe I am looking to deeply into the is but how much thought did Anno put into the characters and their stories?

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon Jun 18, 2012 2:46 pm I guess it's called good writing. Wether you hate or love the characters, they cause a strong reaction, and that's the goal a writer should ask for... well, in my subjective opinion, that is. In any case, there's no point in having a character if the reaction is "meh". You'd be surprised that Anno (The director and main creative driving force) was greatly surprised that the characters got so popular... but then again, even if this guy has some serious self-depreciation going on, there's an important lesson to be learned here: Most awesome characters aren't created to be "awesome" - quite the opposite, you try to make the audience like them, you end up with mary sues since no one likes stuff pushed down their throats. Rather, when you focus on telling a good story and getting the intended message across, the love for the characters happens pretty much by itself. Another part of the secret is, as you yourself have stated, is the degree of realism - They're a bit closer to what actual ppl their age are like, in fact, when I watch EVA, I often think "Hey, that's straight out of a type of real situations I know!" In a way, they represent real typers of people that our society produces, and ppl recognize that, and react, either negetively or positively, depending on whom they resemble and what experiences they've had... Personally, I've found myself reflected in Rei, Mari and some of Misatos monologues about her father, and well... that give me the feeling that I was not alone, and helped me see it all from a new perspective/ describe and understand things i never found the words for... I guess its only natural that I'd get somewhat attached. Another factor is fascination: They're simply interesting, like a puzzle for you to figure out. There's so much detail put into them, down to *philosophic opinions* and favourite colors, and it's not like all those traits are simply dumped together, no, we get an explanation of how it all "flows" from their experiences and basic attributes. Many of them represent various, often seemingly contradictory things within the story, and then there's the way they compliment and contrast each other, the "network" as a whole. The real feat here is that Anno & co. managed to make them feel like real people while, at the same time, having those abstract concepts/ideas behind them. Also, most of the cast simply have both great virtues and fatal flaws combined in one person, (sometimes with both of these being extensions of the same basic personality traits) and the fandom simply has differing opinion as to wether the good parts outweigh the bad ones or not.

[]:             Quote Post by transcendent12 » Mon Jun 18, 2012 7:30 pm I don't really see much of evangelion characters in myself... but I think I gravitate towards people with issues :P That said I found the eva characters growing on me over time. I praise the characterisation because I find it quite original. Yes the templates of the characters may be said to be generic and what not, their depth and presentation is not. I find the characters quite curious in the fact that their core templates hardly change throughout the series but unlike some other anime which achieves this through just lack of development etc. NGE seems to keep loading more and more of the same characterisation. For me this results in characters which have much character depth almost without seeming so. In the end we have extracted an extra dimension of characterisation while on the surface they seem rather 2 dimensional. (e.g. Shinji whiny bitch all the way through with complete anti hero development; Asuka always the angry ego maniac who never lets anyone in her life etc) Of course, everyone has different interpretations but I feel the allure of the series has been simplified inorder to give fans a voice to be able to defend the series in the wake of those who hate it.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Mon Jun 18, 2012 8:12 pm Indeed. I enjoyed their characterization and the way they handled the characters. It just seemed so natural and not forced like I see in most shows. Just how many fix fics and peggy sue fics that are dedicated to "repairing" Shinji's character?

[]: Re: Characterization of Evangelion             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Mon Jun 18, 2012 10:09 pm GAP wrote: I am not on meds or even remotely as depressed as Anno is.

[]:             Quote Post by ReiAyanami25 » Tue Jun 19, 2012 12:07 am I think what makes Eva's characters so strong is that they are all uniquely human. Like it has already been discussed we can find some of all of us reflected in the personalities and actions of Rei, Asuka, Shinji and the other characters.We understand them, and in my case, Eva managed to bring forth and define some emotions I never thought that I possessed. I felt less different and alien, and even though I have been told by many adults that I am self-isolating, I feel comforted knowing that there is a character that is very similar to me. I feel less confused and questioning of myself. For others, this is likely to be a similar case. Plenty of other Anime contain weak characters and poorly devised plots that are only created to satisfy the general public, but Eva does not follow that trend. Unlike most Anime, Eva has succeeded in the act of not jumping on the bandwagon and not seeking to create something that everyone will like and automatically expect. It has succeeded in creating a work of art that raises and defies the expectations of the audience by exhibiting ideas and raising questions that other works of fiction do not. The most successful things in our world are the creations that do not follow a script, they bend what is expected to create something that is spectacular. Shinji, Asuka and Rei and the rest of Eva's characters do just that. I believe that deep down, we all wish to understand, whether the questions involve ourselfves, or other human beings. Anno once said, while pondering the purpose of creating NME: The visual work of Evangelion is composed of a variety of desires. The desire to put our own honest feelings on film. The desire to convey to as many people as possible the fundamental appeal of touching primitive emotions and how versatile the medium of animation is in its expression and images that it is capable of realising... .... The desire to shatter the spreading sense of helplessness. The desire to carry on the strength of spirit that lives on in the real world.... ...Eva is a story of repetition. It is a story where our protagonist faces the same situation many times over and determinately picks himself back up again. It is a story of the will to move forward, even if only a little. It is a story of the resolve to want to be together, even though it is frightening to have contact with others and to endure ambiguous loneliness....

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Tue Jun 19, 2012 1:30 am Anno wrote: The desire to put our own honest feelings on film.

[]:             Quote Post by ReiAyanami25 » Tue Jun 19, 2012 1:35 am Mr. Tines wrote: Honesty, quite brutal at times, is what really makes the characterizations in NGE. The characters are emotionally stripped naked in quite an unsparing manner. The character self-examinations in EoTV are something that it is very difficult to write, because it means putting a lot of your own deep private self onto the page, without the normal figleaves of convention. In many ways that means Anno was doing much the same to himself, suppressing the self-censoring habits we all pick up in just interacting with people.

[]: Re: Characterization of Evangelion             Quote Post by driftking18594 » Tue Jun 19, 2012 10:00 am Warren Peace wrote: Not that I'm accusing you specifically, but Eva fandom tends to have weird ideas about depression. It's generally accepted that, the closer you get to suicide, the greater your writing powers become.

[]:             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Tue Jun 19, 2012 7:45 pm GAP wrote: Indeed. I enjoyed their characterization and the way they handled the characters. It just seemed so natural and not forced like I see in most shows. Just how many fix fics and peggy sue fics that are dedicated to "repairing" Shinji's character?

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Tue Jun 19, 2012 9:39 pm Vegeta 20XX wrote: Fuck if I know. I know Gasmask tried to shoehorn a similar concept with Rei into our RP way back in the day, but since the RP died, it's no longer an issue. The characters are just too broken to fix, unless they have a good decade or two to work on it.

[]: Re: Characterization of Evangelion             Quote Post by Butterscotch » Thu Jun 21, 2012 6:27 pm The characters are really deep and you really connect to them. They are deep and complexed like you would expect in a deep novel. I always wish Evangelion was a novel as it would be amazing to imagine the characters and story in your mind. I understand where people are coming from when they say "I didn't like one character in the series" because each character reminds us of our flaws we don't want to see. The series really helped me understand humans. I agree very powerful writing. Warren Peace wrote: It's generally accepted that, the closer you get to suicide, the greater your writing powers become.
